narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and LSAT score of 33. The admissions model evaluated your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicting a 42.4% chance of failure—high enough that it tipped the decision against admitting you, as the school prioritizes students with stronger projected bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance signal stood out as the top driver, suggesting to the model that your later academic trajectory might not build the momentum needed for bar success. Countering that somewhat was your LSAT score, the second-most important factor, which pulled in the opposite direction toward predicting a pass since 33 is a respectable mark, even if it's below the dataset average of about 37. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, the third key influencer, also nudged the prediction toward passing, though again it's lower than the typical 3.21 average and wasn't strong enough to overcome the Year 3 concerns. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often rank higher in later school years and boast stronger test scores overall—your profile showed promise in standardized testing but was held back by that dip in Year 3 relative performance. This combination made the model view your bar passage odds as riskier than for most applicants. While factors like your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 90%), they didn't sway the outcome much. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as a male applicant from a middle-income background. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted failure risk below the threshold. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these areas—your LSAT foundation gives you a great starting point. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for determined applicants like you.
